title: BM25 Explained: The Classic Ranking Function
seoTitle: BM25 Explained: The Classic Lexical Ranking Function
metaDescription: BM25 is the classic sparse lexical ranking function and the recall backbone of hybrid search. Learn how BM25 scores keyword matches and why it still matters.
oneLiner: You will understand how BM25 scores documents on keyword overlap, why term frequency and document length matter, and why it anchors the sparse side of hybrid search.

blocks.md: **BM25** (its full name is **Okapi BM25**) is a formula that scores how well a document matches a search query, based purely on the *words they share*. Give it a query and a pile of documents, and it ranks every document by a single number: higher score means a better keyword match. It is one of the oldest and most reliable ideas in search, and it still runs underneath products you use every day.

Think of a strict librarian grading essays against a question. The librarian doesn't *understand* your essay the way a person does — they count words. If your essay repeats the key terms from the question, it scores higher. But the librarian is clever about it: repeating a rare, important word like *photosynthesis* counts for a lot, while repeating a common word like *the* counts for almost nothing. And a short, focused essay that hits the keywords beats a rambling 50-page one that mentions them by accident. That balancing act — reward the right words, discount the boring ones, don't let long documents cheat — is exactly what BM25 does with arithmetic.

BM25 is a **lexical** (also called **sparse**) ranker: it matches on the literal characters of words, not their meaning. It has no idea that *car* and *automobile* are related. That sounds like a weakness, and sometimes it is — but it is also why BM25 nails exact matches like error codes, product SKUs, and rare names that meaning-based search often fumbles.

Modern AI search leans heavily on [embeddings](/learn/embeddings-vector-databases/embeddings-explained/what-are-embeddings) — vectors that capture *meaning* so a query about "refund window" can find a passage about "return period" even with no shared words. That is powerful, but it has a blind spot: embeddings are fuzzy. Ask for the exact part number `XR-4471` or the function name `parseUserToken`, and a meaning-based search may shrug and return things that are *about* the same topic instead of the one document that literally contains your string.

BM25 has the opposite strengths and weaknesses, which is precisely why it still matters. It is exact, fast, cheap, and completely explainable — you can point at *why* a document ranked where it did. So in real [RAG](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag) systems, BM25 isn't a relic you replaced with embeddings; it is the partner you run *alongside* them.

items: **Exact-match recall.** BM25 reliably surfaces documents containing rare, literal tokens — codes, IDs, acronyms, names — that dense vectors often miss. It is the safety net for "the user typed the exact thing."
**No training, no GPU.** BM25 needs no model, no embeddings, no GPU. It is just counting and a formula, so it runs on a laptop over millions of documents in milliseconds and costs almost nothing.

**Explainable and stable.** You can trace every score back to which words matched and how rare they were. There is no opaque vector to debug, and the ranking doesn't drift when someone swaps an embedding model.

**The sparse leg of hybrid search.** The consensus production retrieval pattern is [hybrid search](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-hybrid-search): run BM25 *and* embedding search together and fuse the results. BM25 is the recall backbone of the sparse half.

blocks.md: If you build any kind of search or retrieval over text — a help center, a code search, a document store feeding an LLM — knowing BM25 means you understand the baseline that everything else is measured against. A surprising number of "we need fancier search" problems are solved by tuning BM25 first.

BM25 builds its score from three ingredients. None of them is complicated on its own; the cleverness is in how they combine. For each word in the query that also appears in the document, BM25 computes a small contribution, then adds those contributions up.

diagram.title: The three signals BM25 combines per matching term

steps.label: Term frequency (TF)
steps.sub: how often the word is in the doc

steps.label: Inverse doc frequency (IDF)
steps.sub: how rare the word is overall

blocks.md: If a document mentions your query word more times, it is probably more relevant — but only up to a point. A page that says *espresso* 100 times is not 100 times better than one that says it 5 times; it is just a page about espresso. BM25 captures this with **saturation**: the first few occurrences of a word boost the score a lot, and each extra occurrence adds less and less. A tuning knob usually called **k1** controls how quickly that curve flattens out. This is the single biggest improvement BM25 made over the naive "just count the words" approach.

blocks.text: 2. Inverse document frequency, to weight rare words

blocks.md: Not all matched words are equally informative. If a query is *the best espresso machine*, matching on *the* tells you nothing — almost every document contains it. Matching on *espresso* tells you a lot. **Inverse document frequency (IDF)** measures rarity: a word that appears in *few* documents across your whole collection gets a high weight, and a word that appears in *almost every* document gets a weight near zero. So common filler words quietly contribute little, and the rare, meaningful terms dominate the score — without anyone hand-maintaining a stopword list.

blocks.text: 3. Document-length normalization, so short docs compete fairly

blocks.md: A very long document has more chances to contain any given word, purely by accident. Without a correction, long documents would unfairly win every query. BM25 normalizes for length: it compares a document's length to the *average* length in the collection and gently penalizes the ones that are longer than average. A second knob, usually called **b**, controls how strong that penalty is — `b = 1` applies full length normalization, `b = 0` turns it off entirely.

You will see **k1** and **b** in every BM25 implementation. Common defaults are roughly `k1` between 1.2 and 2.0 and `b` around 0.75, and they work well out of the box. Treat them as dials you *can* tune, not knobs you *must* tune — start with defaults and only adjust if evaluation shows a problem.

Put together, BM25 scores a document by walking through each query term, asking "how often is this term here (with diminishing returns), how rare is it overall, and is this document suspiciously long?", and summing the answers. The whole thing is fast because the heavy lifting — knowing which documents contain which words — is precomputed once in an **inverted index** (a map from each word to the list of documents that contain it), so at query time the engine only scores the handful of documents that actually share a word with the query.
Say your collection is a product help center and a user searches for `reset router password`. BM25 looks at only the documents that contain at least one of those three words, then scores each one.

headers: Why it ranks where it does
rows: "How to reset your router password" (short FAQ)
All three rare-ish terms present, document is short
**Top.** Strong term matches, no length penalty — the focused answer wins.
"Router setup guide" (long manual mentioning passwords once)
Only *router* and *password* appear, buried in a long doc
Mid. Fewer matching terms plus a length penalty drag it down.
Lower. Missing the rarest, most specific query term hurts.
"Welcome to our help center" (generic landing page)
Only the common word the-style filler overlaps
Near zero. Common words carry almost no IDF weight.

blocks.md: Notice what BM25 got right with pure counting: the short, exact FAQ beat the long manual, and the document missing the rare word *router* sank even though it matched *reset* and *password*. Notice also what it can't do — if the best answer said "change your gateway login," BM25 would never find it, because it shares no literal words with the query. That gap is exactly where embeddings come in.

blocks.text: BM25 in a few lines of code

blocks.md: You rarely implement BM25 by hand — search engines and libraries ship it. Here is how little code it takes to use a standard library implementation, just to make it concrete.

blocks.title: bm25_quickstart.py
blocks.code:
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi

# Each document is pre-tokenized into a list of words.
corpus = [
    "how to reset your router password",
    "router setup guide and troubleshooting",
    "reset your account password by email",
    "welcome to our help center",
]
tokenized = [doc.split() for doc in corpus]

# Build the index once. BM25 precomputes term stats and doc lengths here.
bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenized)

# Score every document against the query, then take the best matches.
query = "reset router password".split()
scores = bm25.get_scores(query)

ranked = sorted(zip(scores, corpus), reverse=True)
for score, doc in ranked:
    print(f"{score:5.2f}  {doc}")
# The short, exact FAQ scores highest; the generic page scores ~0.

blocks.md: In production you would not loop over every document in Python. A real engine (Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Lucene, Tantivy, or `pgvector`-style hybrid stores) keeps an inverted index and only touches documents that share a term with the query, which is what makes BM25 scale to millions of documents.

sections.title: BM25 vs embeddings: complements, not rivals

blocks.md: The most common mistake is treating BM25 (sparse, lexical) and [embedding search](/learn/embeddings-vector-databases/similarity-search-indexing/what-is-semantic-search) (dense, semantic) as competitors where you pick one. They fail in *opposite* situations, so the strongest systems use both.

columns.title: BM25 (sparse / lexical)
items: Nails exact words: codes, IDs, names
No model, no GPU, no training
Fully explainable scores
Misses synonyms and paraphrases
Strong out-of-the-box baseline

columns.title: Embeddings (dense / semantic)
items: Matches meaning across different words
Needs an embedding model + vector store
Score is an opaque vector distance
Can miss exact rare tokens
Handles synonyms and intent well

blocks.md: Because their strengths are complementary, the standard production pattern is [hybrid search](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-hybrid-search): run BM25 and embedding retrieval in parallel, then merge their two ranked lists. The usual merge method is [Reciprocal Rank Fusion](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/reciprocal-rank-fusion), which combines the lists using each document's *rank* rather than its raw score — so you never have to make BM25 scores and cosine distances live on the same scale. A document that either method ranks highly floats to the top. Many teams then add a [reranker](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-reranking) as a final, precise pass over the fused candidates.

For the conceptual, retrieval-focused take on the same algorithm, see the companion explainer [what is BM25](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-bm25); this page stays on the scoring mechanics.

BM25 is dependable, but a few things quietly determine whether it works well for you. Most of them are about *tokenization* — how text is split into words before BM25 ever sees it — because BM25 can only match tokens you actually produced.

items: **Tokenization is half the battle.** BM25 matches tokens, so how you split, lowercase, and stem text decides what *can* match. If `Router` and `router` become different tokens, or `running` never reduces to `run`, your recall suffers. Match the same analyzer at index time and query time.

**It can't bridge vocabulary gaps.** BM25 has no concept of synonyms. If users say *laptop* and your docs say *notebook*, BM25 finds nothing. Pair it with embeddings (hybrid search) or add synonym expansion at query time.

**Stopwords and tuning are usually fine as-is.** IDF already suppresses common words, so aggressive stopword removal is often unnecessary, and the default `k1`/`b` values are strong. Resist over-tuning before you have an evaluation set that tells you what to fix.

**Very short or very long docs are tricky.** Length normalization helps, but extreme outliers — one-line docs or giant concatenated files — can still behave oddly. Consistent [chunking](/learn/rag/chunking-and-ingestion/what-is-chunking-in-rag) gives BM25 documents of comparable size to score.
**Don't judge it on three queries.** "It looked good on a couple of searches" is not evaluation. Measure retrieval quality on a real query set before deciding BM25 is or isn't enough.

blocks.md: If BM25 returns nothing for a query, it usually means **zero literal token overlap**, not that the document is missing. Before concluding your data is bad, check tokenization and try the query the way it is actually stored. This silent-empty-result trap is the most common BM25 surprise.

BM25 sits in a long lineage. Its predecessor was plain **TF-IDF**, which multiplied term frequency by inverse document frequency but had no saturation curve and no proper length normalization. BM25 added both, which is why it consistently outranked TF-IDF and became the default. The name *Okapi* comes from the Okapi information-retrieval system at City, University of London, where the formula was developed in the 1990s — and decades later it is still the baseline new retrieval methods must beat.

items: **BM25F** scores documents that have *fields* (title, body, tags) by weighting matches differently per field — a title match counts more than a body match.
**BM25+** tweaks the formula so long documents are not over-penalized, fixing an edge case in the original length normalization.

**Learned sparse retrieval** (models in the SPLADE family) keeps BM25's fast inverted-index machinery but lets a neural model decide term weights and expand queries with related terms — a bridge between sparse lexical search and dense embeddings.

blocks.md: Once BM25 makes sense, the natural next steps are the systems that build on it. Learn how it combines with vectors in [hybrid search](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-hybrid-search) and how their results merge via [Reciprocal Rank Fusion](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/reciprocal-rank-fusion). Understand the component that orchestrates all of this — the [retriever](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-a-retriever) — and the precision pass that comes after it, [reranking](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-reranking). Improving the *query* before it ever reaches BM25, via [query rewriting](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/query-rewriting-for-rag), often lifts results more than tuning the ranker itself.

The durable lesson: BM25 is old, simple, and still wins more often than newcomers expect. It is the honest baseline. Before reaching for anything fancier, make sure you can beat a well-tuned BM25 on your own data — and in most real systems, the answer is to keep BM25 in the mix, not replace it.

faq.a: BM25 stands for "Best Matching 25" — it was the 25th in a series of experimental ranking formulas. Its full name is **Okapi BM25**, after the Okapi information-retrieval system where it was developed. The number is just a version label, not a meaningful parameter.

faq.q: Is BM25 better than embeddings for search?
faq.a: Neither is universally better — they fail in opposite situations. BM25 excels at exact keyword and rare-token matches (codes, IDs, names) but ignores synonyms; embeddings match meaning across different words but can miss exact tokens. In practice the best systems run both together as hybrid search rather than choosing one.

faq.q: What are the k1 and b parameters in BM25?
faq.a: `k1` controls term-frequency saturation — how quickly repeated occurrences of a word stop adding to the score. `b` controls document-length normalization — how strongly longer-than-average documents are penalized. Common defaults are roughly `k1` between 1.2 and 2.0 and `b` around 0.75, and they work well without tuning for most collections.

faq.q: Does BM25 understand synonyms or meaning?
faq.a: No. BM25 is a lexical ranker that matches on literal words, so it has no idea that *car* and *automobile* are related. If a relevant document shares no actual words with the query, BM25 will not find it. To bridge that gap you add embedding-based semantic search alongside it.

faq.q: How is BM25 different from TF-IDF?
faq.a: BM25 is an improved descendant of TF-IDF. It adds two things TF-IDF lacks: term-frequency *saturation* (so repeating a word gives diminishing returns) and proper *document-length normalization* (so long documents don't win unfairly). These changes make BM25 consistently rank better, which is why it replaced TF-IDF as the default.

faq.q: Why is BM25 still used when we have embeddings?
faq.a: Because it does things embeddings struggle with: exact matches on rare tokens, near-zero cost with no model or GPU, fully explainable scores, and a rock-solid baseline. It is the recall backbone of the sparse half of hybrid search, so most production retrieval systems keep BM25 in the loop rather than dropping it.
